title: Hummingbird cake
subtitle: A moist banana, pineapple, and pecan layer cake with cream cheese frosting
excerpt: An American Southern layer cake with crushed pineapple, mashed banana, and chopped pecans folded into a spiced oil batter — exceptionally moist and fragrant. Finished with a thick cream cheese frosting.
sourceNotes: The hummingbird cake is attributed to Jamaican origin (a 'Doctor Bird cake' in some early records) but became widely known in the American South from the mid-1970s onwards. The combination of banana, pineapple, and pecan in a spiced oil cake appeared in Southern food writing of the 1970s, and early versions are now in the public domain as recipe features from that era. The formula here follows the classic Southern three-layer approach.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted layers wrapped tightly in cling film. Defrost at room temperature before frosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake layers keep well for 24 hours wrapped at room temperature. The assembled cake keeps refrigerated for 3 days.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cake-making technique where vegetable oil replaces butter as the fat. Oil cakes stay moist longer than butter cakes because oil does not solidify at room temperature. The trade-off is a denser, slightly less complex flavour.

content.text: : no creaming, no waiting for butter to soften, just whisk together dry then wet ingredients and fold to combine. The banana and crushed pineapple add moisture; very ripe bananas are ideal. The pecans go into the batter and as a decoration on the frosted cake.

Preheat the oven to 175°C conventional. Grease and line three 23 cm round tins (or bake in two batches if you have only two). Whisk flour, sugar, bicarbonate of soda, cinnamon, and salt in a large bowl. Make a well in the centre and add the eggs, oil, and vanilla; stir with a spatula just until combined. Fold in the mashed banana, drained pineapple, and chopped pecans. Divide the batter between the tins and bake for 25 to 30 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes, then turn onto racks.

Beat the cream cheese and softened butter together until smooth and no lumps remain. Add the sifted icing sugar in three additions, beating on low speed until incorporated, then increase to medium and beat for 1 minute. Add the vanilla extract. The frosting should be thick enough to spread without running. If it is too soft, refrigerate for 15 to 20 minutes before using.

Ensure the cake layers are fully cool before frosting. Place the first layer on a serving plate and spread a generous amount of frosting over the top. Add the second layer and repeat, then place the third layer on top. Spread the remaining frosting over the top and sides of the cake, using an icing spatula for a smooth or textured finish. Decorate the top with the reserved pecan halves. Refrigerate for 1 hour before serving to allow the frosting to set.
